---
title: "Reproducible reports with Quarto"
toc: true
toc-location: right
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
inline <- function(x = "") paste0("`` `r ", x, "` ``")
```

# Introduction

## Current process

-   Typically people analyse, plot and write up in different programs.

-   Updates to data require manual updates to all the figures and tables as well
    as to text reporting results.

-   Graphs are saved to files and inserted into the final report.

-   Reordering figures, tables and equations means updating everywhere they are
    cross-referenced.

-   Submitting to a different journal requires reformatting the document.

-   Manual labour ..... error prone

## What is Quarto?

-   An open-source scientific and technical publishing system that allows you to
    create dynamic reproducible reports in a variety of output formats.

-   Next generation RMarkdown. Many new features, greater standardisation and
    more flexibility.

    -   Markdown is a lightweight markup language for creating formatted text
        using a plain-text editor. It was designed by [John Gruber in
        2004](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/)

    -   RMarkdown is an extension of Markdown that adds support for embedding R
        code chunks in Markdown documents. It was created by JJ Allaire and
        Yihui Xie early in 2014 but has been worked on by many others since.

-   Mutli-language: Python, R, Julia, and Observable.

-   Include auto-numbered equations, figures and tables that can be cross
    referenced

# Exercises

## Scenario

You are a data analyst for an international NGO. You have been asked to produce
a reproducible country profile report that analyses the relationship between
wealth (GDP per capita) and health (Life Expectancy) over the last 50 years.

## The data

```{r}
#| echo: false
library(tidyverse)

gapminder <- read_tsv("data-raw/gapminder.tsv")



```

We will use data from [Gapminder](https://www.gapminder.org/). Gapminder was
founded in 2005 by Ola Rosling, Anna Rosling Rönnlund and Hans Rosling. It aims
to combat misguided global strategies rooted in misconceptions by promoting a
fact-based worldview grounded reliable statistics. A subset of the data is
[gapminder.tsv](dat-raw/gapminder.tsv). It has `r nrow(gapminder)` rows and
`r ncol(gapminder)` columns (variables). The variables are:
`r paste(colnames(gapminder), collapse = ", ")`[^1]. There are per-capita GDP
and life expectancy data from `r length(unique(gapminder$year))` years (from
`r min(gapminder$year)` to `r max(gapminder$year)`) for each of
`r length(unique(gapminder$country))` countries.

[^1]: Per-capita GDP (Gross domestic product) is given in units
    of [international
    dollars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geary%E2%80%93Khamis_dollar), “a
    hypothetical unit of currency that has the same purchasing power parity that
    the U.S. dollar had in the United States at a given point in time” – 2005,
    in this case.

```{r}
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(gapminder) |> 
  kableExtra::kable_styling() |> 
  kableExtra::scroll_box(height = "200px")
```

## Create a new Quarto document

### Create a new project

🎬 File \| New Project \| New Directory \| Quarto Project

-   Browse to an appropriate place and give your project a name.

-   I used `ngo-report`

-   Choose Engine: Knitr

-   Turn visual markdown editor off (for now)

### Change some RStudio settings

🎬 Tools \| Global Options

-   General:

    -   Turn off the three "Restore ...." options

    -   Turn "Save workspace to .RData on exit" to Never

-   R Markdown:

    -   Turn "Show output preview in:" to Viewer pane

### Your project contains

-   `ngo-report.RProj` - what makes the folder an RStudio project

-   `_quarto.yml` - the configuration file

-   `ngo-report.md` - the main document containing some template text

The code is normal functioning code. You can run it interactively as you write
your report.

🎬 Place your cursor in the code chunk and run it (Ctrl-Enter or use the green
run button)

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

Note that `ngo-report.html` is created and opened in the Viewer pane

## Edit the YAML header

🎬 Add your name, and a title. Also add the engine and format

``` yml
---
title: "The relationship between Gross Domestic Product per capita and Life Expectancy."
author: "Emma Rand"
engine: knitr
format:
  html
---
```

### Add some content

🎬 Add a code chunk (Ctrl-Shift-I) for a simple graph.

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
hist(rnorm(100))
```

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

### Edit the YAML header: default code chunk options

🎬 Set some default code chunk options. I recommend these for reports

``` yml
---
title: "The relationship between Gross Domestic Product per capita and Life Expectancy."
author: "Emma Rand"
engine: knitr
format:
  html
execute:
  echo: false
  include: true
  error: false
  message: false
  warning: false

---
```

🚨 Indentation matters in YAML. Make sure the `execute` options are indented
under `execute`.

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

Code chunk options

-   `echo: false` code will not be included in output
-   `include: true` output will be included
-   `error: false` halt render if a code error occurs
-   `message: false` messages and warnings will not be included
-   `warning: false`

🎬 Experiment with change the options and re-rendering. Try misspelling `rnorm`
and switching between `error: true` and `error: false` to see the error option
in action.

## Adding content

🎬 Delete everything except the YAML header.

### Headings

+-----------------+---------------+
| Markdown Syntax | Output        |
+=================+===============+
| ``` markdown    | # Header 1    |
| # Header 1      |               |
| ```             |               |
+-----------------+---------------+
| ``` markdown    | ## Header 2   |
| ## Header 2     |               |
| ```             |               |
+-----------------+---------------+
| ``` markdown    | ### Header 3  |
| ### Header 3    |               |
| ```             |               |
+-----------------+---------------+
| ``` markdown    | #### Header 4 |
| #### Header 4   |               |
| ```             |               |
+-----------------+---------------+

🎬 Add headings for: Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion, and References

🎬 Add a code chunk for package loading

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
#| label: load-packages
library(tidyverse)
```

`#| label: load-packages` is a code chunk label. These are useful (here) and
essential (later) for cross-referencing.

### Formatting text

+---------------------+---------------------+
| Markdown Syntax     | Output              |
+=====================+=====================+
| ``` markdown        | *italics*, **bold** |
| *italics*,**bold**  |                     |
| ```                 |                     |
+---------------------+---------------------+
| ``` markdown        | ***bold italics***  |
| ***bold italics *** |                     |
| ```                 |                     |
+---------------------+---------------------+
| ``` markdown        | superscript^2^      |
| superscript^2^      |                     |
| ```                 |                     |
+---------------------+---------------------+
| ``` markdown        | subscript~2~        |
| subscript~2~        |                     |
| ```                 |                     |
+---------------------+---------------------+
| ``` markdown        | ~~strikethrough~~   |
| ~~strikethrough~~   |                     |
| ```                 |                     |
+---------------------+---------------------+
| ``` markdown        | `verbatim code`     |
| `verbatim code`     |                     |
| ```                 |                     |
+---------------------+---------------------+

### Links & Images

+------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| Markdown Syntax              | Output                                   |
+==============================+==========================================+
| ``` markdown                 | [Quarto](https://quarto.org)             |
| [Quarto](https://quarto.org) |                                          |
| ```                          |                                          |
+------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| ``` markdown                 | ![Caption](images/logo.png){width="130"} |
| ![Caption](images/logo.png)  |                                          |
| ```                          |                                          |
+------------------------------+------------------------------------------+

🎬 Add this text to the Introduction

::: blockquote
There is a relationship between wealth and health. We will explore this using
the relationship between Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per capita and Life
Expectancy across countries and over time.
:::

🎬 Make the "per capita" italic

🎬 Make "Gross Domestic Product (GDP)" a link to
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gross_domestic_product>

### Data import

🎬 Make a folder called `data-raw`

🎬 Save [gapminder.tsv](data-raw/gapminder.tsv) to `data-raw`

🎬 Add a code chunk with:

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
#| label: import-data
file <- "data-raw/gapminder.tsv"
gapminder <- read_tsv(file)
```

🎬 Run chunks interactively

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

### Methods text

🎬 Add this text to Methods:

::: blockquote
We took per capita GDP and life expectancy data from 12 years (from 1952 to
2007) for each of 142 countries. Analysis was carried out with R and tidyverse
packages.
:::

It would be good not to have "hard coded" those numbers in the text. What if we
get more data? Or want to make a report for a subset of the data?

## Inline code: reproducible reporting

-   Inline code is how you include a variable value, like a sample size, mean or
    statistical result, in a section of text.

-   In fact, any code output can be inserted directly into the text of a .qmd
    file using inline code.

Inline code goes between \`r\` and \` .

For example by writing:

The squareroot of 2 is \`r `sqrt(2)` \`

you will get:

The squareroot of 2 is `r sqrt(2)`

### Extract summary information for use in inline code

🎬 Add a code chunk with:

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
#| label: extract-summary
# extract values for inline reporting
n_years <- length(unique(gapminder$year))
min_year <- min(gapminder$year)
max_year <- max(gapminder$year)
n_counties <- length(unique(gapminder$country))

```

🎬 Replace numbers in the Methods text with inline code:

::: blockquote
We took per capita GDP and life expectancy data from `r inline('n_years')` years
(from `r inline('min_year')` to `r inline('max_year')`) for each of
`r inline('n_counties')` countries. Analysis was carried out with R and
tidyverse packages.
:::

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

## Tables

Suppose we want to open our results section with information about the five most
populated countries in `r max_year`. We can extract this information and report
it in a table.

🎬 Add a small piece of text to the results section:

::: blockquote
Data about about the five most populated countries in `r inline('max_year')` is
shown in Table 1.
:::

🎬 Add a code chunk with:

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
#| label: max-year-top-pop
max_year_top_pop <- gapminder |> 
  filter(year == max_year) |> 
  arrange(desc(pop)) |> 
  slice_head(n = 5) 
```

If you run the code chunk interactively, you will see the data frame
`max_year_pop_pop` in the Environment pane. Highlighting and executing the name
`max_year_pop_pop` will show the data frame in qmd.

If you want the table to appear in the rendered output you need to print.

🎬 Add a code chunk with:

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
#| label: max-year-top-pop-print
max_year_top_pop
```

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

As you can see, this is not a very nice table. We can use the `kable` function
from the `knitr`[^2] to make a nice table.

[^2]: There are several packages for formatting tables in R such as
    [**`gt`**](https://gt.rstudio.com/) and
    [**`formattable`**](https://renkun-ken.github.io/formattable/) for HTML
    tables and
    [**`flextable`**](https://davidgohel.github.io/flextable/index.html) if you
    have to render to Word

🎬 Replace the `max_year_top_pop` in the previous code chunk with:

```         
max_year_top_pop |> 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Table 1. Five most populated countries in 2007.")
```

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

You now have a nice table with a caption!

We have written Table 1 in manually. This is not ideal because we might want to
add/remove tables and cross reference them in the text and would not want to
manually update all the places where the table is mentioned.

Quarto has a great feature for naming, autonumbering and cross referencing
tables. We simply have to include `tbl-` in the chunk label.

🎬 Edit the chunk like this:

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
#| label: tbl-max-year-top-pop
max_year_top_pop |> 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Five most populated countries in 2007.")
```

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

To cross reference we can refer to the table in the text with
`@tbl-max-year-top-pop`.

🎬 Edit the text to say:

::: blockquote
Data about about the five most populated countries in is shown in
`@tbl-max-year-top-pop`
:::

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

::: {.callout-important title="Challenge"}
Can you add a table (and referring text) that shows the five most wealthy
(gdpPercap) countries in `r max_year`? You will need to create new code chunks
to extract the data and to print the table. Remember to include `tbl-` in the
label of the code chunk that prints the table and to refer to it in the text
with `@` followed by the chunk label.
:::

## Figures

Figures can be included in a similar way. The label of the code chunk which
produces the figure is important. Just as we had to use `tbl-` in the chunk
label to have output numbered as a table, we need to use `fig-` in the chunk
label to have output numbered as a figure.

We will make a bubble chart showing the relationship between GDP per capita and
life expectancy in `r max_year`. The size of the bubbles will represent
population and the colour will represent continent.

🎬 Add a code chunk to the Results section to filter the data for the most
recent year:

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
#| label: subset-max-year

# Filter the dataset to the most recent year for our report
gapminder_max_year <- gapminder |> 
  filter(year == max_year)
```

🎬 Now add the code for the figure. Make sure to include `fig-` in the chunk
label

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
#| label: fig-gdp-life-max-year

# Create a bubble chart
ggplot(gapminder_max_year, aes(x = gdpPercap, 
                               y = lifeExp, 
                               color = continent, 
                               size = pop/10^6)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_size_continuous(name = "Population\n(millions)") +
  scale_color_discrete(name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Gross Domestic Product per capita") +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Life Expectancy (years)") +
  theme_classic()
```

🎬 Add a sentence with a cross reference to the figure:

::: blockquote
The relationship between Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per capita and Life
Expectancy across countries in `r max_year` is in @fig-gdp-life-max-year.
:::

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

## Figure legends

Our figure has a number (because we used `fig-` in the chunk label) but it does
not have a legend. There are two ways to add a legend:

1.  Using the `fig-cap` chunk option

2.  Using a div `:::`

Divs are more flexible and their use extends beyond figure legends. I will show
both methods.

### A figure legend with `fig-cap`

🎬 Add the `fig-cap` chunk option:

```{r}
#| echo: fenced

#| fig-cap: "The relationship between Gross Domestic Product (GDP) 
#|           per capita and Life Expectancy across countries 
#|           in 2007."
# Create a bubble chart
# ...

```

You will notice that we do not need to number the figure ourselves. The figure
is automatically numbered as Figure 1 and the legend is automatically updated to
include the figure number.

### A figure legend with a div

Instead of using the `fig-cap` chunk option, we can use a div to create a figure
legend. This is more flexible than `fig-cap` because:

-   You can include inline code, citations, special characters and formatting
-   You can more easily make multipanel figures

🎬 Add a div by:

-   removing the `fig-cap` option **and** the `label` option

-   adding `:::` before and after the code chunk

-   placing the label after the opening `:::` like this: `:::{#fig-gdp-life}`
    Note the`#` is important for numbering

-   Putting the legend text between the code chunk and the closing `:::`

You can use this for legend text:

::: blockquote
**The relationship between Gross Domestic Product (GDP) *per capita* and Life
Expectancy across countries `r inline("max_year")`.** Each point represents a
country, plotted by gross domestic product (GDP) *per capita* (international
dollars, x-axis) against life expectancy at birth in years (y-axis). Point
colour indicates continental region and point size is proportional to population
size (millions). Data are from the Gapminder dataset and were analysed with R
and tidyverse packages.
:::

::: {.callout-important title="Challenge"}
Can you add a figure that shows the relationship between Gross Domestic Product
(GDP) *per capita* and Life Expectancy across countries in `r min_year` You will
need to create new code chunks to subset the data and make the figure. Add the
figure legend with a div. Remember to include `fig-` in the div label and to
refer to it in the text with `@` followed by the chunk label.
:::

## The Visual editor!

-   The visual editor is a WYSIWYG editor for Quarto documents. It can be
    accessed by clicking the `Visual Editor` button in the top right or by
    Ctrl-Shift-F4

-   It makes it easier to add and edit content (although I find I often want to
    use the source)

-   It is especially useful for adding citations.

## Citations

Quarto will automatically generate citations and a bibliography in a number of
styles. To use this capability, you need:

-   the citation to be formatted in markdown `@key` where `key` is the citation
    key for the reference you want to cite. The citation key is defined in the
    bibliography file (see below) and is often the first author’s surname
    followed by the year of publication, for example `@pollack_should_2007`.

-   A bibliographic data source, for example a BibLaTeX (.bib) or BibTeX
    (.bibtex) file.

-   Optionally, a CSL file which specifies the formatting to use when generating
    the citations and bibliography.

The easiest way to add a citation is to use the visual editor which allows you
to add citations from a variety of sources including: by doi, from Zotero and by
searches of [Crossref](https://www.crossref.org/),
[DataCite](https://datacite.org/), or
[PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/).

🎬 Switch to visual using the button or (Control-Shift-F4)

Go to the methods section where we have this text:

::: blockquote
Analysis was carried out with R and tidyverse packages.
:::

🎬 Put your cursor after tidyverse and do Insert \| Citation (Control-Shift-F8)

🎬 Chose R Package and search for tidyverse

Note that it says (towards the end) that it will Create a bibliography file
called `references.bib` and add the citation to it.

🎬 Click Insert

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

Ta da! You have a citation in the text and a bibliography at the end of the
document.

Open `references.bib` and you will see the BibTex entry for tidyverse. You can
edit the BibTex entry to add other references to `references.bib`:

-   by copying and pasting BibTex entries manually from other sources.
-   using the Insert \| Citation (Control-Shift-F8) option and adding by doi,
    through a search, very usefully, a Zotero library if you have it set up.

We will add the reference for R itself. You can find the reference for R using
`citation()`:

🎬 Get the reference for the version of R you are using:

```{r}
citation()
```

🎬 Copy the BibTex entry (starts with `@` and ends with `}` ) and paste it into
`references.bib`

🎬 Edit in a `key` (I used `R-core`) and save.

🎬 Put your cursor after R, do Insert \| Citation (Control-Shift-F8), choose
Bibliography and add `R-core` and Insert.

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

We are now going to use an article by @pollack_should_2007 as a reference for
this statement:

::: blockquote
There is a relationship between wealth and health
:::

This article is available here:

<https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0749379707003145?via%3Dihub>

If you are using a reference manager you may be using a browser extension to
save the article. Otherwise Click Cite and export to BibTex.

Copy the contents of the BibTex file and paste it into `references.bib` in your
project folder. Edit the entry to add a key (I used `pollack_should_2007`) and
save.

🎬 Put your cursor after "wealth and health", do Insert \| Citation
(Control-Shift-F8), choose Bibliography and add `pollack_should_2007` and
Insert.

If you have any trouble getting hold of the BibTex entry, you can save
[pollack_should_2007.bib](pollack_should_2007.bib) and copy the contents into
`references.bib` in your project folder.

🎬 Hit Render (Ctrl-Shift-K)

## PDF output

## Parameterised reports

Pages made with R [@R-core], Quarto [@Allaire_Quarto_2026], `knitr` [@knitr1;
@knitr2; @knitr3], `kableExtra` [@kableExtra]

# References
